# Document 316: Respond to Google Chat app commands  |  Google for Developers

Source URL: https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/commands
Product area: chat-developers
Original file: docs/respond-to-google-chat-app-commands-google-for-developers-2f843fbf.md

# Respond to Google Chat App Commands — Summary

**Source:** Google Developers — <https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/commands>  
**Last updated:** 2026-04-20 UTC

## Purpose

This guide explains how to **set up and respond to commands** in a Google Chat app.

> Commands help users discover and use key features of a Chat app. Only Chat apps can see the content of a command. For example, if a user sends a message with a slash command, the message is only visible to the user and the Chat app.

Google recommends using commands as part of designing user journeys for Chat apps.

---

## Types of Google Chat App Commands

Google Chat apps can define three command types:

| Command type | How users invoke it | Best used when |
|---|---|---|
| **Slash command** | Type `/` followed by predefined text, such as `/about`, or select from the command menu | The app needs additional user input |
| **Quick command** | Open the command menu from the reply area, click **Add**, then select a command | The app can respond immediately without extra input |
| **Message action** | Hover over a message, open the three-dot menu, and select an action | The app performs an action based on a specific message |

### Slash Commands

Users type a slash command such as:

```text
/about
/search receipts
```

Use slash commands when the app needs argument text from the user.

Example:

> Create a slash command called `/search` that runs after the user enters a phrase to search for, like `/search receipts`.

---

### Quick Commands

Users select quick commands from the command menu in the message reply area.

Use quick commands when the app can respond immediately.

Example:

> Create a quick command called **Random image** that responds immediately with an image.

---

### Message Actions

> **Developer Preview**

Users invoke message actions from the message context menu.

Use message actions when the app can act on the context of a message.

Example:

> Create a message action if your Chat app can perform actions based on the context of a message.

---

## Prerequisites

You need a **Google Chat app that receives and responds to interaction events**.

Google provides quickstarts depending on implementation language/platform:

- **Node.js**
- **Apps Script**
- **Python**
- **Java**

For HTTP-service apps, Google points to the Google Cloud Functions Chat app quickstart.  
For Apps Script apps, Google points to the Apps Script Chat app quickstart.

---

# Set Up a Command

Setting up a command involves two main steps:

1. **Create a name and description**
2. **Configure the command in the Google Cloud console**

---

## Naming and Describing Commands

The command name is what users type or select to invoke the Chat app. A short description appears below the command name to help users understand how to use it.

### Naming Recommendations

Use names that are:

- **Short**
- **Descriptive**
- **Actionable**
- Easy for users to understand

Example recommendation:

```text
Use "Remind me" instead of "Create a reminder"
```

Additional guidance:

- Use common names for common interactions, such as:
  - `Settings`
  - `Feedback`
- Use unique names when possible to avoid confusion with commands from other Chat apps.
- If multiple apps use the same command name, users may need to filter through similar commands.

---

### Description Recommendations

Descriptions should be:

- Short
- Clear
- Explicit about expected behavior

Include formatting requirements when relevant.

Example:

```text
Remind me to do [something] at [time]
```

Also clarify whether the app responds:

- Publicly to everyone in the space
- Privately to the user who invoked the command

Example:

```text
Learn about this app (Only visible to you)
```

---

# Configure a Command in Google Cloud Console

To create a **slash command**, **quick command**, or **message action**, configure it in the Google Chat API settings for your app.

## Steps

1. In Google Cloud console, go to:

   ```text
   Menu > APIs & Services > Enabled APIs & Services > Google Chat API
   ```

   Google Chat API page: <https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/api/chat.googleapis.com>

2. Click **Configuration**.

3. Under **Commands**, click **Add a command**.

4. Enter command details:

   | Field | Details |
   |---|---|
   | **Command ID** | A number from `1` to `1000`; used by the Chat app to recognize the command and return a response |
   | **Description** | Up to 50 characters; can include special characters |
   | **Command type** | Choose **Quick command**, **Slash command**, or **Message action** |
   | **Command name** | Depends on the command type |

---

## Command Name Rules by Type

### Quick Command Name

- Display name users select from the menu
- Up to 50 characters
- Can include special characters

Example:

```text
Remind me
```

---

### Slash Command Name

- Text users type in a message
- Must start with `/`
- Must contain only text
- Up to 50 characters

Example:

```text
/remindMe

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

---

# Document 317: Respond to Google Chat app commands  |  Google for Developers

# Respond to Google Chat App Commands — Markdown Summary

**Source:** Google Developers — Google Chat  
**URL:** https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/slash-commands  
**Last updated:** 2026-04-20 UTC

---

## Overview

Google Chat app commands help users discover and use key features of a Chat app. Commands are visible only to the user and the Chat app, not to everyone in the space.

> “Only Chat apps can see the content of a command. For example, if a user sends a message with a slash command, the message is only visible to the user and the Chat app.”

Before building commands, Google recommends designing the user journey first: [Define all user journeys](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/journeys).

---

## Types of Google Chat App Commands

Google Chat apps support three command types:

| Command type | How users invoke it | Best used when |
|---|---|---|
| **Slash commands** | Type `/` plus predefined text, such as `/about`, or select from the command menu | The app needs additional user input |
| **Quick commands** | Open the command menu from the message reply area, click **Add**, and select a command | The app can respond immediately without more input |
| **Message actions** | Hover over a message, open the three-dot menu, and select an action | The app acts on the context of an existing message |

---

### Slash Commands

Users invoke slash commands by typing a slash (`/`) followed by predefined text, such as:

```text
/about
/search receipts
```

Use slash commands when your app needs arguments or additional input.

Example use case:

> Create a slash command called `/search` that runs after the user enters a phrase to search for, like `/search receipts`.

---

### Quick Commands

Users invoke quick commands from the Chat message reply area command menu.

Use quick commands when the app can respond immediately.

Example:

> Create a quick command called **Random image** that responds immediately with an image.

---

### Message Actions

> **Developer Preview**

Users invoke message actions from the three-dot menu on a specific message.

Use message actions when the app performs an action based on the context of a message.

Example:

> A message context menu can contain a **“Remind me”** message action.

---

## Prerequisites

You need a Google Chat app that receives and responds to [interaction events](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/receive-respond-interactions).

Supported implementation paths:

- **Node.js**
  - Create an interactive Chat app using an HTTP service with the [Google Cloud Functions quickstart](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/quickstart/gcf-app).
- **Apps Script**
  - Create an interactive Chat app using the [Apps Script quickstart](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/quickstart/apps-script-app).
- **Python**
  - Create an interactive Chat app using an HTTP service with the [Google Cloud Functions quickstart](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/quickstart/gcf-app).
- **Java**
  - Create an interactive Chat app using an HTTP service with the [Google Cloud Functions quickstart](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/quickstart/gcf-app).

---

# Set Up a Command

Setting up a command involves:

1. Creating a command **name and description**
2. Configuring the command in the **Google Cloud console**

---

## Name and Describe the Command

The command name is what users type or select to invoke the Chat app. A short description appears below the command name to help users understand how to use it.

---

### Command Naming Recommendations

Use names that are:

- **Short**
- **Descriptive**
- **Actionable**

Example:

```text
Remind me
```

Instead of:

```text
Create a reminder
```

Additional guidance:

- Use common names for familiar features, such as:
  - `Settings`
  - `Feedback`
- Use unique names when possible to avoid confusion with similar commands from other Chat apps.

> If your command name is the same as a command from another Chat app, users must filter through similar commands to find yours.

---

### Command Description Recommendations

Descriptions should be short and clear.

They should explain:

- What the command does
- Whether formatting is required
- Whether the response is visible to everyone or only the invoking user

Example for a slash command requiring arguments:

```text
Remind me to do [something] at [time]
```

Example for a private quick command:

```text
Learn about this app (Only visible to you)
```

---

## Configure the Command in Google Cloud Console

To configure slash commands, quick commands, or message actions:

1. In the Google Cloud console, go to:

   ```text
   Menu > APIs & Services > Enabled APIs & Services > Google Chat API
   ```

   Or open: [Google Chat API page](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/api/chat.googleapis.com)

2. Click **Configuration**.

3. Under **Commands**, click **Add a command**.

4. Enter the command details:

   - **Command ID**
     - A number fr

# Document 318: Respond to incidents with Google Chat, Vertex AI, and Google Apps Script  |  Google for Developers

Source URL: https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/tutorial-incident-response-app-auth
Product area: chat-developers
Original file: docs/respond-to-incidents-with-google-chat-vertex-ai-and-google-apps-script-google-for-de-0d52e2d9.md

# Respond to Incidents with Google Chat, Vertex AI, and Google Apps Script — Summary

**Source:** Google for Developers — Google Chat tutorial  
**URL:** https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/tutorial-incident-response-app-auth  
**Last updated:** 2026-04-20 UTC

---

## Overview

This tutorial explains how to build a **Google Chat incident response app** using:

- **Google Chat API**
- **Vertex AI**
- **Google Apps Script**
- **Google Docs API**
- **Admin SDK Directory API**
- **Google Workspace Marketplace SDK**

The app responds to incidents in real time by:

- Creating a dedicated Google Chat space for an incident.
- Adding incident responders to the space.
- Posting an initial incident summary.
- Supporting collaboration using Chat messages, slash commands, and dialogs.
- Using **Vertex AI** to summarize the incident discussion.
- Creating a **Google Docs post-mortem** containing the resolution, summary, and full Chat history.
- Sharing the generated document back into the Chat space.

> This tutorial uses authorization scopes that begins with  
> `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.app.*`.  
> For the Chat app to use those scopes, you must get a one-time administrator approval.

---

## Incident Definition and Example Scenarios

An **incident** is an event requiring immediate team attention.

Examples include:

- A time-sensitive CRM case requiring service team collaboration.
- A system outage requiring SREs to coordinate recovery.
- A high-magnitude earthquake requiring emergency response coordination.

For this tutorial, incidents are initiated from a simulated external web page where users enter:

- Incident title
- Incident description / initial message
- Email addresses of responders

---

## App Flow

The tutorial demonstrates the app through these UI stages:

1. Website where someone reports an incident.
2. Notification that the incident Chat space is created.
3. Incident response Chat space.
4. Resolving the incident with a slash command.
5. Incident resolution dialog.
6. Google Docs post-mortem shared in the space.
7. AI-generated incident summary in the Google Doc.

---

## Prerequisites

If these are not enabled, a Google Workspace administrator must enable them.

### Required Account and Services

- A **Business or Enterprise Google Workspace account**
- Access to **Google Chat**
- **Directory/contact sharing** enabled for Google Workspace

### User Requirements

Incident responders must:

- Be users in the same Google Workspace organization.
- Have Google Chat accounts.

The app uses the Directory API to look up responder information such as:

- Name
- Email address
- User ID

---

## Objectives

The tutorial’s goals are to build a Chat app that:

- Responds to incidents.
- Creates incident response spaces.
- Posts incident and response messages.
- Uses interactive Chat app features.
- Summarizes conversations and resolutions with **Vertex AI**.

---

## Architecture

The app uses both Google Workspace and Google Cloud resources.

### High-Level Architecture Flow

1. A user starts an incident from an external website hosted on Apps Script.
2. The website sends an asynchronous HTTP request to the Chat app, also hosted on Apps Script.
3. The Chat app processes the request:
   - Uses Apps Script Admin SDK service to get team member info.
   - Uses Chat API through Apps Script Advanced Chat service to:
     - Create a Chat space.
     - Add team members.
     - Send an incident message.
4. Team members discuss the incident in the Chat space.
5. A team member invokes the `/closeIncident` slash command.
6. The app:
   - Lists all Chat messages in the space.
   - Sends the messages to Vertex AI.
   - Generates a summary.
   - Creates a Google Docs document using Apps Script `DocumentApp`.
   - Posts the document link back to the Chat space.

---

## Prepare the Environment

### 1. Create a Google Cloud Project

You can create the project using either the Google API Console or `gcloud`.

#### Using `gcloud`

```bash
gcloud projects create PROJECT_ID
```

Replace `PROJECT_ID` with the desired project ID.

---

### 2. Enable Billing

#### List billing accounts

```bash
gcloud billing accounts list
```

#### Link billing account to project

```bash
gcloud billing projects link PROJECT_ID --billing-account=BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID
```

Replace:

- `PROJECT_ID` — your Google Cloud project ID.
- `BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID` — the billing account ID to link.

---

### 3. Enable Required APIs

Enable the following APIs:

- Google Chat API
- Google Docs API
- Admin SDK API
- Google Workspace Marketplace SDK
- Vertex AI API

#### Using `gcloud`

Set current project:

```bash
gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID
```

Enable APIs:

```bash
gcloud services enable chat.googleapis.com docs.googleapis.com admin.googleapis.com aiplatform.googleapis.com appsmarket-component.googleapis.com
```

---

## Authentication and Authorization

The app uses different credentials depending on the API:

| API / Service | Credentia

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

# Respond to Incidents with Google Chat, Vertex AI, Apps Script, and User Authentication

**Source:** Google Developers — Google Workspace add-ons  
**URL:** https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/tutorial-incident-response  
**Last updated:** 2026-05-29 UTC

---

## Overview

This tutorial shows how to build a **Google Chat incident response app** using:

- **Google Chat API**
- **Vertex AI**
- **Google Apps Script**
- **Google Docs**
- **Admin SDK Directory API**
- **User authentication**

The app helps teams respond to incidents in real time by:

- Creating a dedicated Google Chat space for an incident.
- Adding responders to the space.
- Posting incident details.
- Supporting a `Close incident` quick command.
- Opening a dialog to capture resolution details.
- Reading the Chat conversation.
- Summarizing the response with **Vertex AI Gemini**.
- Creating a Google Docs post-mortem.
- Sharing the generated document back into the Chat space.

> An _incident_ is an event that requires the immediate attention of a team of people to resolve.

Examples include:

- A time-sensitive CRM case requiring service-team collaboration.
- A system outage requiring SRE response.
- A high-magnitude earthquake requiring emergency coordination.

For the tutorial, an incident starts when a user submits a form on a web page with:

- Incident title
- Incident description / initial message
- Email addresses of responders

---

## What the App Does

The incident management app workflow:

1. A user opens an Apps Script-hosted web page.
2. The user submits incident details.
3. The app creates a Google Chat space.
4. The app adds responders and itself to the space.
5. The app posts the initial incident message.
6. Responders discuss the incident.
7. A responder uses the quick command **Close incident**.
8. The app opens a dialog asking for resolution details.
9. The app retrieves up to the first 100 Chat messages.
10. Vertex AI summarizes the conversation.
11. The app creates a Google Docs post-mortem.
12. The app posts the document link in the Chat space.

---

## Prerequisites

If needed, ask a Google Workspace administrator to enable these:

- A **Business or Enterprise Google Workspace account** with access to **Google Chat**.
- **Directory / contact sharing** enabled for Google Workspace.

Important notes:

- Incident responders must be users with a Google Chat account in the same Google Workspace organization.
- The app uses the Directory API to look up responder contact information such as names and email addresses.

---

## Objectives

The tutorial’s goals are to build a Chat app that can:

- Respond to incidents.
- Create incident-response Chat spaces.
- Post incident and response messages.
- Support interactive Chat app features such as:
  - Messages
  - Button clicks
  - Quick commands
  - Dialogs
- Summarize conversations and resolutions with **Vertex AI**.

---

## Architecture

The app uses Google Workspace and Google Cloud resources:

1. A user starts an incident from an external Apps Script-hosted website.
2. The website sends an asynchronous HTTP request to the Chat app, also hosted on Apps Script.
3. The Chat app:
   - Uses Apps Script Admin SDK service to get team member information.
   - Uses Apps Script Advanced Chat service / Chat API to:
     - Create a Chat space.
     - Add team members.
     - Send a message.
4. Team members discuss the incident in the Chat space.
5. A team member invokes the `Close incident` quick command.
6. The app:
   - Lists messages in the Chat space.
   - Sends the conversation to Vertex AI for summarization.
   - Creates a Google Docs document using `DocumentApp`.
   - Posts a Chat message linking to the document.

---

4. Select the Cloud Billing account.
5. Click **Set account**.

```bash
gcloud billing accounts list
```

```bash
gcloud billing projects link PROJECT_ID --billing-account=BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID
```

Replace:

- `PROJECT_ID` — your Cloud project ID.
- `BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID` — your billing account ID.

---

Required APIs:

- Google Chat API
- Google Docs API
- Admin SDK API
- Vertex AI API

### Using gcloud CLI

Set the current project:

```bash
gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID
```

Enable APIs:

```bash
gclou

# Scale AI adoption with AI Boost Bootcamp

Source: https://workspace.google.com/blog/ai-and-machine-learning/scale-ai-adoption-with-ai-boost-bootcamp?hl=en

AI and Machine Learning

# Scale AI adoption with AI Boost Bootcamp

April 8, 2026

![https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/26836_WS_Boost_Bites_AI_Skilling_Blog_Hero.max-2600x2600.png](https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/26836_WS_Boost_Bites_AI_Skilling_Blog_Hero.max-2600x2600.png)

##### Deanne Chen

Google Workspace with Gemini Product Marketing Manager

##### Try Google Workspace at No Cost

Get a business email, all the storage you need, video conferencing, and more.

[SIGN UP](https://workspace.google.com/business/signup/welcome?source=gafb-blog_article-body-en)

To help your teams upskill and get more from their AI tools, we’re introducing [AI Boost Bootcamp](https://www.skills.google/course_templates/1674), a new training series available on [Google Skills](http://skills.google/) — our new home for AI learning and more. The training is designed to:

- Accelerate AI and agentic work transformation by training your teams in under 50 minutes.
- Make it easier to track your progress on Google Skills.

### **Closing the AI skills gap**

Buying AI tools is easy; integrating them into your teams’ daily work to fuel transformation is the real hurdle. According to a [recent Deloitte study](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/what-we-do/capabilities/applied-artificial-intelligence/content/state-of-ai-in-the-enterprise.html), closing the AI skills gap is now a top priority, with 53% of companies investing in AI fluency and 48% on upskilling workers for applying AI in their roles.

We designed the AI Boost Bootcamp series to help close that gap, allowing teams to scale AI learning and adoption across their entire organizations. We’re kicking off the series with a course that’s designed for knowledge workers: [**How to Use Gemini at Work 101**](https://www.skills.google/course_templates/1674). Here’s what is covers:

- Overview and how to find Gemini across Google's tools for work
- Project management and planning with Gemini and NotebookLM
- Presentations and charts with Gemini in Slides, Sheets, and more
- Aligning stakeholders across meetings with AI notetaking, scheduling, and more
- Communicating with speed and precision using *Help Me Write* in Gmail, and Gemini Gems for repetitive tasks
- Getting started with AI agents and agentic work with Gemini Enterprise

### **How we designed the course**

AI Boost Bootcamp is based on Google’s own proven, internal training and builds on [AI Boost Bites](https://cloud.google.com/transform/introducing-ai-boost-bites-from-google?e=48754805), which offers quick, actionable tips on AI productivity. We heard from customers that they’re looking for AI best practices bundled as a concise, practical learning journey. AI Boost Bootcamp covers the most important knowledge you need to make the most of Google’s AI tools for work, such as Google Workspace with Gemini, NotebookLM, the Gemini app, and Gemini Enterprise.

### **Track progress and earn credentials**

For a structured learning experience, every learner can track their progress and test their knowledge on [**Google Skills**](https://www.skills.google/course_templates/1674). When they’ve successfully completed the series, they’ll earn an official skill badge to share on social media to showcase their proficiency.

Help your whole team amplify their potential with [AI Boost Bootcamp](https://www.skills.google/course_templates/1674).

# Document 322: Schedule actions in Gemini Apps - Computer - Gemini Apps Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/16316416
Product area: gemini-apps
Original file: docs/schedule-actions-in-gemini-apps-computer-gemini-apps-help-a07f5f83.md

# Schedule Actions in Gemini Apps — Summary

**Source:** [Google Gemini Apps Help — Schedule actions in Gemini Apps](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/16316416)  
**Platform covered:** Computer / web app

## Overview

Gemini Apps can schedule recurring actions as part of daily, weekly, or monthly routines. Scheduled actions let Gemini generate content automatically at a chosen time, such as news digests, creative inspiration, skill-learning suggestions, calendar summaries, weather forecasts, and more.

Gemini prepares scheduled content in the background so it is ready by the delivery time.

---

## Key Excerpts

> “You can make Gemini a part of your daily, weekly, and monthly routines by scheduling recurring actions.”

> “You can set a delivery time that works for your schedule. Gemini will prepare your content in the background so it’s ready by the scheduled time.”

> **Important:** You can have up to 10 active scheduled actions at a time.

> “Gemini will prepare your content within the hour leading up to your delivery time to help ensure you get the latest information.”

> “Responses for scheduled actions are prepared in advance to guarantee timely delivery. As a result, rapidly changing data, like stock prices, won’t be the latest when you receive them.”

> “Scheduled actions work best for daily summaries and wrap-ups.”

---

## Requirements

To schedule actions in Gemini Apps, you must meet **both** of the following requirements:

### 1. Sign in with an eligible account

You need one of these account types:

- A **personal Google Account** with a **Google AI Pro or Ultra subscription**
- A **work or school Google Account** with access to a **qualifying edition of Google Workspace**

Relevant links from the article:

- [Learn how to upgrade](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/14517446)
- [Qualifying Google Workspace editions](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/14620100)
- [Learn how to sign in to Gemini Apps](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/13278668)

### 2. Keep Activity must be on

- **Keep Activity** must be enabled.
- Scheduled actions are **not available** when Keep Activity is off.

Link:

- [Keep Activity](https://myactivity.google.com/product/gemini?utm_source=help)

---

## How to Schedule an Action

### Steps

1. Go to [gemini.google.com](https://gemini.google.com/).
2. In the text box at the bottom, enter your prompt.
   - Include details about **when** and **how often** Gemini should run the action.
3. Click **Submit**.
4. Gemini replies with a summary of the scheduled action.

### Prompting tip

When creating a scheduled action, explicitly state the schedule and instructions, such as:

- Daily at a specific time
- Weekly on a certain day
- Monthly on a recurring date
- What information Gemini should include
- Whether it should use connected apps like Gmail, Calendar, or Docs

---

## Active Scheduled Action Limit

You can have:

- **Up to 10 active scheduled actions at a time**

Paused actions may remain saved, but only active ones count as running scheduled actions.

---

## Timing and Data Freshness

Gemini prepares scheduled content **within the hour before the delivery time**.

Important limitation:

- Scheduled actions are prepared in advance to ensure they arrive on time.
- Because of this, rapidly changing data may not be fully current when delivered.

Examples of data that may not be latest:

- Stock prices
- Cryptocurrency prices
- Other fast-changing market or live data

Best use cases:

- Daily summaries
- Daily wrap-ups
- News digests
- Calendar or email summaries
- Recurring learning prompts
- Weather and planning assistance

---

## Editing a Scheduled Action

You can update a scheduled action in two ways:

### Option 1: Ask Gemini in the scheduled action chat

In the chat associated with the scheduled action, ask Gemini to change the schedule or instructions. Changes are saved for future runs.

### Option 2: Use Gemini settings

1. Go to [gemini.google.com](https://gemini.google.com/).
2. Click **Settings & help**.
3. Select **Scheduled actions**.
4. Find the scheduled action.
5. Click **More** → **Edit**.
6. Update the scheduled action.
7. Click **Save**.

---

## Deleting a Scheduled Action

To delete a scheduled action:

1. Go to [gemini.google.com](https://gemini.google.com/).
2. Click **Settings & help**.
3. Select **Scheduled actions**.
4. Find the scheduled action.
5. Click **More** → **Delete**.

---

## Pausing or Resuming a Scheduled Action

You can pause an action without deleting it.

### What happens when paused

If an action is paused:

- Gemini will not run the prompt.
- The scheduled action remains saved.
- You can resume it later.

### Steps to pause or resume

1. Go to [gemini.google.com](https://gemini.google.com/).
2. Click **Settings & help**.
3. Select **Scheduled actions**.
4. Find the scheduled action.
5. Toggle it:
   - Turn it **off** to pause.
   - Turn it **on** to resume.

---

## Scheduled Action Notifications

Gemini n

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

---

# Document 323: Search & retrieve your files in Drive with Gemini - Computer - Google Drive Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/drive/answer/16685111
Product area: drive
Original file: docs/search-retrieve-your-files-in-drive-with-gemini-computer-google-drive-help-db5b3384.md

# Search & Retrieve Files in Google Drive with Gemini — Summary

**Source:** Google Drive Help — “Search & retrieve your files in Drive with Gemini”  
**URL:** https://support.google.com/drive/answer/16685111  
**Platform covered:** Computer

---

## Key Requirement

> **Important**: To use Gemini in Drive, you must have an eligible Google Workspace or Google AI plan.

Gemini in Drive is available only with eligible plans. Google links to additional information about Gemini features and plan eligibility.

---

## What Gemini in Drive Does

Gemini lets you search Google Drive using **natural language** instead of relying on exact file names, keywords, or manual review of search results.

You can ask Gemini to:

- Find specific files or folders
- Locate information across Google Workspace
- Search files shared through other Workspace apps
- Generate summarized answers from file contents via **AI Overviews**
- Continue research in the full-screen **Ask Gemini** workspace

Google notes that users should review its Gemini disclaimers and data usage information to understand how data is used and what to expect from Gemini suggestions.

---

## Search with Natural Language

You can type everyday-language prompts into the Drive search bar to locate files across your Workspace.

### Steps

1. On your computer, go to `drive.google.com`.
2. Enter a natural-language prompt in the search bar.
3. Click inline citations to verify the source files.

### Example Prompts

> `"Show me the slide decks about the project timeline that Deepti shared with me in Chat."`

> `"Show me the PDF Josh emailed me last week about the marketing strategy."`

Gemini can find files not only in Drive but also from shared Workspace contexts such as Chat or Gmail, depending on enabled search scope.

---

## Get Answers Directly in Search with AI Overviews

When you ask a question about your files, Gemini may show an **AI Overview** at the top of Drive search results.

This overview summarizes an answer using information from your files.

### Steps

1. Go to [drive.google.com](https://drive.google.com/).
2. Enter a question in the search bar.
3. Review the AI Overview at the top of search results.
4. Click inline citations to verify the source files.

### Example Questions

> `"What is the Q3 marketing budget?"`

> `"Summarize the ABC project timeline for this year."`

---

## Continue in the Full-Screen Ask Gemini Workspace

After receiving an AI Overview, you can move into the full-screen **Ask Gemini** workspace for follow-up work.

### What You Can Do There

- Ask follow-up questions
- Compare findings
- Turn research into a saved project

### Steps

1. From the search overview, click **Ask Gemini**.
2. The conversation opens in the full-screen workspace.
3. Your current search context and source files carry over automatically.

> **Tip**: You can provide feedback on the overview by clicking thumbs up or thumbs down.

---

## Control AI Overview Search Scope

You can choose which Google Workspace sources Gemini uses for:

- AI Overviews
- Natural-language file finding

### Steps

1. Go to [drive.google.com](https://drive.google.com/).
2. In the top search bar, click **AI Overview search**.
3. Check or uncheck sources to include or exclude.

### Available Sources

- Drive files
- Gmail attachments & links
- Chat uploads & links
- Calendar attachments & links

> **Tip:** These settings only apply to Gemini AI Overviews and natural language file finding. They do not affect your standard Drive search results.

---

## Related Google Help Resources

- [Get started with Gemini in Google Drive](https://support.google.com/drive/answer/16686008)
- [Use Gemini in Drive for research & analysis](https://support.google.com/drive/answer/16963068)
- [Share your Gemini research using Drive projects](https://support.google.com/drive/answer/16684520)

---

## Additional Help Options

Google also provides:

- Help Community support
- Contact support flow
- Google Help YouTube tutorials
- Option to give feedback on the article

Feedback categories include:

- Inaccurate
- Hard to understand
- Missing info
- Irrelevant
- Minor errors
- Other suggestions

# Document 324: Search with Google Lens in Chrome - Computer - Google Chrome Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/15086890
Product area: chrome-ai
Original file: docs/search-with-google-lens-in-chrome-computer-google-chrome-help-59e3cbdc.md

# Search with Google Lens in Chrome — Summary

**Source:** Google Chrome Help — “Search with Google Lens in Chrome”  
**URL:** https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/15086890  
**Platform covered:** Computer

## Overview

Google Lens in Chrome lets you search or ask questions about content on a webpage. You can use it to identify objects, find products, search images, highlight text, select areas of a page, or ask questions about the current page.

> “You can search anything you find with Google Lens in Chrome. For example, you can select an image of a shirt on a page to know where to buy it or take a photo of an animal to identify it.”

---

## Requirement

**Important:** You must have **Google set as your default search engine**.

> **Important:** [Set Google as your default search engine](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/95426).

---

## Search a Page with Google Lens

### Steps

1. Open **Chrome** on your computer.
2. Go to a website.
3. At the top right, select **More** → **Search this tab with Google Lens**.
   - Alternatively, right-click on a page and select **Search this tab with Google Lens**.
4. Use Google Lens to interact with the page.

---

## What You Can Do with Google Lens

### Ask Questions About the Current Page

You can ask questions about the page you’re viewing.

> **Ask questions about the content of the page you’re on:** At the top right of the screen, in the search box or on the side panel, enter your question. The answers to your question are provided by Google Lens based on the content of the page.

### Select Anything to Search

You can search by clicking, highlighting text, or dragging over part of the page.

> **Select anything to search:** On a webpage, click, highlight text, or select and drag anywhere on the page.

### Ask Follow-Up Questions

To continue the conversation or refine your search:

> To ask a follow-up question, enter it in the "Ask a follow up" bar near the bottom of the side panel. This leads you to AI Mode.

Google provides a related help article for AI Mode:  
[Learn how to get AI-powered responses with AI mode in Google Search](https://support.google.com/websearch/answer/16011537)

### AI Overviews

Search results may include AI-generated summaries.

> You may find AI Overviews in your Google Search results.

Related article:  
[Learn about AI Overviews](https://support.google.com/websearch/answer/14901683)

---

## Closing the Google Lens Side Panel

You can close the side panel in any of these ways:

- Select anywhere in the live page area.
- Press **Esc**.
- In the side panel, select **Close**.

---

## Search Results Behavior

- Search results appear in the **side panel**.
- To open a result separately, select the search result to open it in a **new tab**.

> Search results display in the side panel. To open a result in a new tab, select the search result.

---

## Pin Google Lens to the Toolbar

You can pin **Search this tab with Google Lens** to the Chrome toolbar for quicker access.

> In the toolbar, you can pin "Search this tab with Google Lens."

Related article:  
[Learn how to pin and unpin a specific side panel](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/13156494#pinthesearchbar)

---

# Additional Ways to Search with Google Lens

## From the Address Bar

### Steps

1. Open **Chrome** on your computer.
2. Go to a website.
3. Select the **address bar** → **Ask Google about this page**.

### Add Tabs or Files to a Question

To include more context:

> To add tabs or files to your question, select **AI Mode**.

### Address Bar Tips

- **“Ask Google about this page”** may appear by default on certain pages.
- To check AI Mode history:
  - From the address bar, select **AI Mode** → **View your AI Mode history**.

---

## From an Image

### Steps

1. Right-click the image.
2. Select **Search with Google Lens**.

You can also right-click outside an image and choose **Search with Google Lens** from the menu.

---

# How Page Data Is Processed

Google explains how data is handled when you use Lens in Chrome.

## When Searching Webpage Content

- A **screenshot of the page** and **page data** are sent to Google.
- Google does **not save data beyond temporary storage** needed to process the specific query.
- The page or image is **not used for training** as a result of the Lens query.

> When you use Lens to search webpage content in Chrome, a screenshot of the page and page data will be sent to Google. Google doesn't save any data beyond temporary storage for processing the specific query. Neither the page nor image is used for training as a result of this.

## When Selecting Part of a Page

- The selected page content is sent to **Google Search**.
- You can manage or delete Search activity in **My Google Activity**.

> When you select part of the page, the selection is sent to Google Search. To manage or delete your Search activity, go to [My Google Activity](https://myactivity.google.com/myactivity).

---

# Related Resources

- [Search the we

# Document 325: Search your browsing history in Chrome with AI - Google Chrome Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/15305774
Product area: chrome-ai
Original file: docs/search-your-browsing-history-in-chrome-with-ai-google-chrome-help-c54a0584.md

# Search Your Browsing History in Chrome with AI — Summary

**Source:** Google Chrome Help  
**URL:** https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/15305774

## Overview

Chrome’s **History search, powered by AI** lets you search your browsing history using everyday language, even if you don’t remember the exact keyword, page title, or website address.

It can return AI-generated “best match” results based on sites you previously visited.

---

## Requirements

To use this feature:

- You must be **located in the US**
- Chrome must be set to **English**

Google links to instructions for changing Chrome languages or translating pages.

---

## Key Excerpts

> You can use everyday language to search your browsing history and find sites you visited. This works even if you don't know an exact keyword or website address.

> When you turn on "History search, powered by AI," in addition to the page title and URL, the page contents of the website you browse at that time are stored locally.

> Only the websites you visit after you turn on this feature can be used by AI-powered history and may appear in your AI best matches.

> As you browse websites, their page contents are saved and encrypted to your device and might affect Chrome’s performance.

> Website content can change over time. Even if the website content has changed since your last visit, AI-powered history search results use the content present at the time when it was browsed.

---

## Manage “History Search, Powered by AI”

### Important Notes

When the feature is enabled:

- Chrome stores more than standard history:
  - **Page title**
  - **URL**
  - **Page contents from websites you browse**
- Page contents are:
  - Stored **locally**
  - Saved and **encrypted to your device**
- Only pages visited **after enabling** the feature can be used for AI-powered history search.
- AI best matches may include those later-visited pages.
- The feature may be unavailable or disabled if you use:
  - A **managed profile**
  - A **managed browser**
- Organizations may disable the feature.
- Saving and encrypting page contents may affect **Chrome performance**.

### Turn the Feature On or Off

1. On your computer, open **Chrome**.
2. At the top right, select **More** → **Settings** → **AI Innovations**.
3. Select **History search, powered by AI**.
4. Turn **History search, powered by AI** on or off.

**Tip:** You can turn off “History search, powered by AI” at any time in Chrome Settings.

---

## Find AI Best Match Answers from Browsing History

### Important Behavior

AI-powered history search uses the version of page content that existed **when you visited the page**, not necessarily the current version of the website.

This means results may reflect older content if the website has changed since your visit.

---

## How to Search History with AI

1. On your computer, open **Chrome**.
2. At the top right, select **More** → **History** → **History** page.
3. Enter your search.
4. Check the results.

### Search Tips for AI-Powered Results

To increase the chance of receiving AI-powered history results:

- Make your search text or question **precise and detailed**
- Use **2 or more words**
- Describe what you’re searching for in everyday language

### Time Filters

You can filter history results by:

- **Yesterday**
- **Last 7 days**
- **Last 30 days**

---

## Best Matches from Browsing History

AI-powered results appear under:

> “Best matches from your history”

Details:

- This section displays AI-powered best matches.
- It can show **up to 3 best matches**.

---

## Limitations and Result Behavior

- Short, simple searches are matched directly to:
  - Page title
  - URL
- Short/simple searches will **not** produce AI-powered results.
- AI-powered results only use content from sites visited after the feature was enabled.
- Results are based on page contents stored at the time of browsing.

---

## Feedback on AI Best Matches

You can rate AI best match results:

- Select **Thumbs up** if the result was helpful.
- Select **Thumbs down** if the result did not meet your needs.

If you select **Thumbs down**, you can provide additional feedback explaining why the result was not useful.

---

## Other Ways to Use History Search

### Address Bar

You can also search browsing history from the **Chrome address bar**.

Related help page:  
[Learn how to find a page from your history](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/95589#history_search)

### Side Panel

To use **History search, powered by AI** in the side panel, you can pin **History** to your toolbar.

Related help page:  
[Learn how to pin and unpin a specific side panel](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/13156494#pinthesearchbar)

---

## Terms of Service

Use of **History search, powered by AI** is subject to Google’s terms.

> You agree that your use of History search, powered by AI is subject to the [Google Terms of Service](https://policies.google.com/terms).

---

## Related Resources

- [Check or delete 

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

# Document 326: Search your Chrome history with Gemini in Chrome - Google Chrome Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/16716225
Product area: chrome-ai
Original file: docs/search-your-chrome-history-with-gemini-in-chrome-google-chrome-help-eb72192a.md

[Skip to main content](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/16716225#search-form)

# Search your Chrome history with Gemini in Chrome

You can find pages you've visited before in Chrome by asking Gemini in Chrome. You don’t need to keep tabs open to save them for later, or remember the exact site and when you visited it. Just describe what you're searching for using everyday language, like "what’s that chicken parmesan recipe I found last week?" Gemini in Chrome will search your Chrome history to help you rediscover that page and resume what you were doing.

## What you need

This feature is only available if you:

- Opted in to Gemini in Chrome. [Learn how to get Gemini in Chrome](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/16283624).
- Updated to the latest version of Chrome. [Learn how to update Chrome](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/95414).
- Signed in to Chrome with a personal Google Account. [Learn how to sign in to Chrome](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/185277).

  - This feature isn’t available in Incognito mode. It’s also not available if you sign in to Chrome with a work or school Google Account.
- Synced your Chrome history. [Learn how to sync your history in Chrome](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/185277).

**Important:** For now, this feature is not available in Live chats with Gemini in Chrome.

## Find pages you visited in Chrome

1. On your computer, open Chrome.
2. At the top of your browser, click Ask Gemini ![](https://storage.googleapis.com/support-kms-prod/DOo8pSHet4pMKNJy8JqVa95JUbHs0U63uHqg).
3. Ask Gemini in Chrome to find a page you previously visited.
   - Use everyday language to describe the page you’re trying to find.

[Learn how to check or delete your Chrome browsing history](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/95589).

### Examples

- Show me the article I was reading this morning about the stock market.
- What was that place with vegan pizza I was looking at?
- What was that hotel I was looking at in Palm Springs last week?
- What were the steps for that DIY guide to fixing a leaky faucet?
- Show me those ladies trousers I was looking at.

## Gemini in Chrome can’t find the page

If you asked Gemini in Chrome to find a page you previously visited but it couldn’t find it:

1. Make sure you’re signed in to the same Google Account you used when you visited the page. This should also be the same Google Account that you use with Gemini.
2. If you use Chrome on multiple devices, check if your Chrome history is synced. [Learn how to sync your history in Chrome](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/165139).

## How your data is handled

Learn more about [what happens to your data when you use Gemini in Chrome](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/13594961#chrome_data).

## Related resources

- [Use Gemini in Chrome](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/16283624)
- [Go Live with Gemini in Chrome](https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/16363185)
- [Check or delete your Chrome browsing history](https://support.google.com/chrome/answer/95589)

# Document 327: Send a message using the Google Chat API  |  Google for Developers

Source URL: https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/create-messages
Product area: chat-developers
Original file: docs/send-a-message-using-the-google-chat-api-google-for-developers-70b8f442.md

# Send a Message Using the Google Chat API — Comprehensive Summary

**Source:** Google for Developers — Google Chat API  
**URL:** https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/create-messages  
**Last updated:** 2026-05-08 UTC

---

## Overview

The Google Chat API lets developers use the [`create()` / `CreateMessage()`](https://developers.google.com/workspace/chat/api/reference/rpc/google.chat.v1#google.chat.v1.ChatService.CreateMessage) method on the `Message` resource to create messages in Google Chat.

You can use it to:

- Send messages containing:
  - Plain text
  - Rich text
  - Cards
  - Interactive widgets
- Send private messages visible only to a specific user.
- Start or reply to message threads.
- Assign custom IDs to messages for later retrieval, update, or deletion.
- Quote existing messages.
- Send messages as:
  - A **Chat app**
  - A **Chat app on behalf of a user**

> **Maximum message size:**  
> The maximum message size, including text and cards, is **32,000 bytes**.  
> If a message exceeds this size, the Chat app must send multiple messages.

Chat apps can also send messages synchronously in response to user interactions without calling the Chat API, such as posting a welcome message when added to a space.

---

## How Google Chat Displays API-Created Messages

The way Chat displays and attributes a message depends on the authentication type.

### App Authentication

When using **app authentication**, the Chat app sends the message directly.

- Chat displays `App` next to the sender name.
- The message is attributed to the Chat app.
- Supports rich message features:
  - Text
  - Cards
  - Interactive widgets
  - Accessory widgets
  - Private messages
  - Forced notifications
  - Silent messages

> With app authentication, the Chat app sends the message. To note that the sender isn't a person, Chat displays `App` next to its name.

### User Authentication

When using **user authentication**, the Chat app sends the message on behalf of the user.

- Chat displays the user as the sender.
- Chat also displays the Chat app name next to the user’s name.
- Generally supports **text messages only**.
- In **Developer Preview**, user-authenticated messages can also include cards.

> With user authentication, the user sends the message, and Chat displays the Chat app name next to the user's name.

---

## Prerequisites

To use the examples in the guide, you need:

- A **Business or Enterprise Google Workspace account** with access to Google Chat.
- A Google Cloud project.
- OAuth consent screen configured.
- Google Chat API enabled and configured with:
  - Chat app name
  - Icon
  - Description
- A Google Chat space where:
  - The authenticated user is a member, or
  - The calling Chat app is a member.
- Appropriate credentials depending on authentication type:
  - **User authentication:** OAuth client ID credentials saved as `credentials.json`
  - **App authentication:** Service account credentials saved as `credentials.json`
- Appropriate authorization scopes.
- Language-specific setup:
  - Node.js Cloud Client Library
  - Python Cloud Client Library
  - Java Cloud Client Library
  - Apps Script project with Advanced Chat Service enabled

For app authentication, the Chat app must be added to the space.

---

## Authentication Scopes

Common scopes mentioned:

### App Authentication

```text
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.bot
```

### User Authentication

```text
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.create
```

---

## Sending a Message as the Chat App

Using app authentication, a Chat app can send messages with rich content, including text, cards, and interactive accessory widgets.

Required request fields:

- `chat.bot` authorization scope
- `parent`: the target `Space` resource, such as `spaces/SPACE_NAME`
- `message`: the `Message` resource to create

Message content can include:

- `text`
- `cardsV2`
- Both `text` and `cardsV2`

Optional fields include:

- `accessoryWidgets`
- `privateMessageViewer`
- `messageId`
- `thread.threadKey`
- `messageReplyOption`

### Representative Node.js Example

```js
import {createClientWithAppCredentials} from './authentication-utils.js';

// This sample shows how to create message with app credential
async function main() {
  // Create a client
  const chatClient = createClientWithAppCredentials();

  // Initialize request argument(s)
  const request = {
    // Replace SPACE_NAME here.
    parent: 'spaces/SPACE_NAME',
    message: {
      text:
        '👋🌎 Hello world! I created this message by calling ' +
        "the Chat API's `messages.create()` method.",
      cardsV2: [
        {
          card: {
            header: {
              title: 'About this message',
              imageUrl:
                'https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/i/short-term/release/googlesymbols/info/default/24px.svg',
            },
            sections: [
              {
                header: 'Contents',
                widgets: [
                  {

# Document 328: Set up activity alerts for Workspace Studio  |  Google Workspace Help

Source URL: https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/studio/set-up-activity-alerts-for-workspace-studio
Product area: workspace-studio-admin
Original file: docs/set-up-activity-alerts-for-workspace-studio-google-workspace-help-ff969952.md

# Set up activity alerts for Workspace Studio — Summary

**Source:** Google Workspace Help  
**URL:** https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/studio/set-up-activity-alerts-for-workspace-studio  
**Last updated:** 2026-06-11 UTC

---

## Overview

Administrators can create activity alerts for **Google Workspace Studio flow activity** in the **Workspace Alert Center**. Alerts can notify admins by **email** or in the **Admin Console**.

These alerts are useful for monitoring situations such as:

- Flows running unusually often
- Potential loops or misconfigurations
- Excessive quota consumption
- Heavy usage of AI-powered steps such as **Ask Gemini**

> “As an administrator, you can get alerts about Google Workspace Studio flow activity, such as when flows run a lot or use a lot of AI steps.”

Alerts are configured through **Rules** in the Google Admin console.

---

## Prerequisites

To create these activity rules:

- Go to the Google Admin console.
- Navigate to **Menu → Rules**.
- You need the **View Trust Rules administrator privilege**.
- The data source **Workspace Studio log events** must be available.
  - If it does not appear, verify that:
    - Your Google Workspace edition supports it.
    - You have privileges to view Workspace Studio event logs.

Direct link:

> [Go to Rules](https://admin.google.com/ac/ax)

---

# Example 1: Alert for High Flow Activity

## Purpose

Create an activity rule to notify administrators when Workspace Studio flows run an unusually high number of times in a short period.

This helps identify:

- Potential loops
- Misconfigurations
- Quota consumption issues

## Alert Trigger

This example sends a notification when the **Start run** event occurs more than **100 times in one hour**.

> “This example shows how to create an activity rule that sends a notification when the ‘Start run’ event occurs more than 100 times in one hour.”

---

## Steps

1. In the Google Admin console, go to:

   **Menu → Rules**

   Direct link:

   > [Go to Rules](https://admin.google.com/ac/ax)

2. At the top of the rules table, click:

   **Create rule → Activity**

3. Enter a **Rule name**, for example:

   > `High Workspace Studio flow activity`

4. Optionally enter a **Description**, for example:

   > `Alert when flows run more than 100 times in an hour.`

5. Click **Continue**.

6. Click **Data source** and select:

   > `Workspace Studio log events`

7. Add a condition:

   - **Attribute:** `Event`
   - **Operator:** `Is`
   - **Value:** `Start run`

8. Click **Continue**.

9. In **Action and notification**, select:

   > **If the event frequency meets a specific threshold**

10. Configure the frequency threshold:

    - **Count:** `100`
    - **Time period:** `1 Hour`

    Meaning:

    > “more than 100 times in 1 hour”

11. Select where to send notifications and choose a severity level.

12. Click **Continue**.

13. Verify the settings:

    - **Source:** Workspace Studio log events
    - **Conditions:** Event Is Start run
    - **Threshold:** If the event happens more than 100 times in 1 hour
    - **Alerts:** On, if notifications in the Alert center were selected
    - **Emails:** On, if email notifications were selected
    - **Rule status:** Active

14. Click **Create Rule**.

---

# Example 2: Alert When Flows Use Gemini a Lot

## Purpose

Create an activity rule to notify administrators when Workspace Studio flows use the **Ask Gemini** step frequently.

This helps track usage of the AI-powered **Ask Gemini** step.

> “You can set up an activity rule to notify administrators when Workspace Studio flows use the **Ask Gemini** step.”

---

## Alert Trigger

This example sends a notification when **Ask Gemini** steps are used more than **1000 times in 24 hours**.

> “This example shows how to create an activity rule that sends a notification when these AI-powered steps are used more than 1000 times in 24 hours.”

---

## Steps

1. In the Google Admin console, go to:

   **Menu → Rules**

   Direct link:

   > [Go to Rules](https://admin.google.com/ac/ax)

2. At the top of the rules table, click:

   **Create rule → Activity**

3. Enter a **Rule name**, for example:

   > `Workspace Studio - Ask Gemini Alert`

4. Optionally enter a **Description**, for example:

   > `Alert when flows use Ask Gemini more than 1000 times in 24 hours.`

5. Click **Continue**.

6. Click **Data source** and select:

   > `Workspace Studio log events`

7. Add the first condition:

   - **Attribute:** `Event`
   - **Operator:** `Is`
   - **Value:** `Start step`

8. Click **Add condition** and add a second condition:

   - **Attribute:** `Step name`
   - **Operator:** `Contains`
   - **Value:** `Ask Gemini`

9. Click **Continue**.

10. Select where to send notifications and choose a severity level.

11. Click **Continue**.

12. Verify the settings:

    - **Source:** Workspace Studio log events
    - **Conditions:** Event Is **Start step** AND Step Name Contains **Ask Gemini**
    - **Threshold:** If the

# Document 329: Start with a great prompt - Google Workspace Learning Center

Source URL: https://support.google.com/a/users/answer/14590328
Product area: workspace-learning-center
Original file: docs/start-with-a-great-prompt-google-workspace-learning-center-5e6c1431.md

# Start with a great prompt — Google Workspace Learning Center Summary

**Source:** Google Workspace Learning Center  
**URL:** https://support.google.com/a/users/answer/14590328  
**Topic:** Getting started with Gemini prompts in Google Workspace apps

---

## Core Message

Prompts are instructions you give to **Gemini** to generate content. The quality of Gemini’s output depends heavily on the quality of the prompt. Strong prompts use **natural language**, provide **clear instructions**, and include enough **context or specific details** for Gemini to produce a useful response.

> “Prompts tell Gemini what to do. And the better the prompt, the better the result.”

Gemini prompts can be used across Google Workspace apps, including:

- **Google Docs**
- **Google Sheets**
- **Google Slides**
- **Gmail**
- **Google Meet**
- **Google Vids**
- **gemini.google.com**

---

## Availability Note

This feature is available only if enabled by your organization.

> “You can use this feature only if your organization supports it. For help, contact your administrator.”

---

## Important Usage Warnings Before Using Gemini

Google provides several cautions for using Gemini in Google Workspace:

- Gemini feature suggestions **do not represent Google’s views** and should not be attributed to Google.
- Do **not** rely on Gemini features for:
  - Medical advice
  - Legal advice
  - Financial advice
  - Other professional advice
- Gemini may suggest **inaccurate or inappropriate information**.
- User feedback helps improve Gemini’s helpfulness and safety.
- Enterprise users can submit feedback about generative AI features, but:
  - Feedback should not contain **personal, sensitive, or confidential information**
  - Feedback may be **read by humans**

---

## What Is a Prompt?

A prompt is the instruction or request you give Gemini.

> “A prompt tells Gemini what you want it to generate.”

Good prompts should:

- Use **natural language**
- Sound like instructions to a **friend or colleague**
- Include relevant **context**
- Provide **specific details**
- Clearly state the desired result

> “Good prompts use natural language, as if you were giving instructions to a friend or colleague. And the best prompts provide the context, or the specific details, Gemini needs to provide a great response.”

---

# How to Write Prompts in Google Workspace Apps

---

## Write a Prompt in Google Docs

Use Gemini in Docs through **Help me write**.

### Steps

1. On your computer, open a [Google Docs file](https://docs.google.com/document/).
2. Click where you want to write in the document.
3. On the left, click **Help me write**.
4. Enter a prompt with your instructions.
5. Click **Create**.
6. Review Gemini’s response.
7. Optional: Click **Refine** and choose how Gemini should change the content.
8. When finished, click **Insert**.

### Example Prompt

```text
Imagine you are the lead on a project. Write an update to your executive leadership team on [Project X]. Include the current status for each business function and next steps. Use a formal, brief tone.
```

### Related Help

- [Learn more at the Docs Editors Help Center](https://support.google.com/docs/answer/13951448)

---

## Write a Prompt in Google Sheets

Use Gemini in Sheets through the **Ask Gemini** sidebar.

### Steps

1. On your computer, open a new spreadsheet or tab in [Google Sheets](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/u/0/).
2. The **Ask Gemini** sidebar opens automatically.
3. Enter a prompt.
4. Click **Create**.
5. Optional: Click **Refine** and choose how Gemini should change the content.
6. When finished, in the preview, click **Insert**.

### Example Prompt

```text
Imagine you are preparing for a customer meeting with a new client. Create a framework for researching the client's company in order to create a sales prospect.
```

### Related Help

- [Learn more at the Docs Editors Help Center](https://support.google.com/docs/answer/14356410)

---

## Write a Prompt in Google Slides

Use Gemini in Slides to create images.

### Steps

1. On your computer, open a presentation in [Google Slides](https://docs.google.com/presentation/).
2. Click the slide where you want to add the image.
3. Go to **Insert → Image → Create image with Gemini AI**.
4. In the right-side panel, enter a prompt.
5. Optional: Click **Add a style** to customize the image.
6. Click **Create** to see several suggested images.
7. Optional:
   - **Edit your prompt:** Click the prompt at the top of the right panel, revise it, then click **Create**.
   - **See more suggested images:** Click **View more**.
8. When finished, click **Insert**.

### Prompting Tip for Better Images

For better image-generation results, include details such as:

- Subject
- Setting
- Distance to subject
- Materials
- Background

### Example Prompt

```text
A close up of a boat made out of wood on a lake surrounded by trees at sunrise.
```

### Related Help

- [Learn more at the Docs Editors Help Center](https://support.google.com

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

# Document 330: Stop a Workspace Studio flow as an admin  |  Google Workspace Help

Source URL: https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/studio/stop-a-workspace-studio-flow-as-an-admin
Product area: workspace-studio-admin
Original file: docs/stop-a-workspace-studio-flow-as-an-admin-google-workspace-help-3d78d2df.md

# Stop a Workspace Studio Flow as an Admin — Summary

**Source:** Google Workspace Help  
**URL:** https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/studio/stop-a-workspace-studio-flow-as-an-admin  
**Last updated:** 2026-06-11 UTC

---

## Purpose

Google Workspace Studio lets users create **flows** that automate work across Google Workspace and allowed third-party services. While flows can improve productivity, they may sometimes behave in **unexpected or unwanted ways**.

Admins can stop problematic flows by:

1. Identifying which flow is causing the issue.
2. Contacting Google Workspace Support to stop that specific flow.
3. If urgent, temporarily disabling **all Workspace Studio flows** for the flow owner.

---

## Key Excerpts

> With Google Workspace Studio, people in your organization can create flows to automate work across Workspace and third-party services they're allowed to connect to.

> These flows can make people more productive by automating routine tasks, but they can sometimes work in unexpected or unwanted ways.

> To stop a flow, first you figure out which flow is causing the problem, then send a request to Support to stop it.

> **Important:** These steps turn off all the user's flows. Make sure that they don't have any business critical flows before you continue.

---

## How Unwanted Flow Activity Is Usually Discovered

Admins typically detect problematic Studio flow activity through:

- **User reports**
- **Workspace event logs**

Examples of suspicious or unwanted activity include:

### High Frequency Activity

Examples:

- More Gmail messages sent than normal
- More Google Chat messages sent than normal
- More file changes than expected
- More files created than would be possible for a human user

**Tip:** Chat messages sent by Studio flows include a **chip next to the sender’s name** showing they were sent by a Studio flow.

### Regularly Scheduled Unwanted Activity

Examples:

- Activity repeating approximately every hour
- Activity occurring at all times of day

**Note:** Flows have built-in staggering, so repeated activity may not happen at exactly the same interval each time.

---

## Step 1: Find Which Flow Is Causing the Problem

Use **Workspace Studio log events** in either:

- **Audit and investigation tool**
- **Security investigation tool**

Reference: [Workspace Studio log events](https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/reports/workspace-studio-log-events)

### Recommended Attribute Filters

Use these filters when searching Studio logs:

#### Actor

Set the **Actor** filter to the affected user’s email address.

#### Event

If you know when the problem occurred:

- Filter by **Step complete**
- Look for a step that finished around the same time as the unwanted activity

You may also filter by **Step app** if you know where the activity occurred, such as:

- Gmail
- Google Chat
- Another Workspace app

### Record the Flow ID

Once you identify the flow in the results list, note the:

- **Flow ID**

This is required when contacting Google Workspace Support.

---

## Step 2: Stop a User’s Flow

### Preferred Method: Contact Google Workspace Support

Contact Google Workspace Support and provide the **Flow ID** for the flow you want stopped.

Support will stop the specific flow for you.

Reference: [Contact Google Workspace support](https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/support/contact-google-workspace-support)

---

## Urgent Option: Stop All Flows Owned by the User

If the flow must be stopped immediately and you cannot wait for Support, you can stop **all** flows owned by that user.

> **Important:** These steps turn off all the user's flows. Make sure that they don't have any business critical flows before you continue.

### Steps

1. **Add an organizational unit**
   - Set the user’s current organizational unit as the parent.
   - Reference: [Add an organizational unit](https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/users/advanced/add-an-organizational-unit)

2. **Move the user to the new organizational unit**
   - Reference: [Move the user to an organizational unit](https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/users/advanced/move-users-to-an-organizational-unit)

3. In the **Google Admin console**, go to:

   ```text
   Menu > Apps > Google Workspace > Workspace Studio
   ```

   Direct link: [Go to Workspace Studio](https://admin.google.com/ac/managedsettings/243976345647)

   **Required privilege:**  
   [Service Settings administrator privilege](https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/users/administrator-privilege-definitions#service_settings)

4. Click **Service status**.

5. On the left, select the new organizational unit.

6. Change **Service status** to **Off**.

7. Click **Override**.

8. After Support stops the problematic flow:
   - Move the user back to their original organizational unit.
   - The user can use Workspace Studio again.
   - The stopped flow remains off until the user manually turns it back on.

---

## Important Notes and Cautions

-

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

# Document 331: Suggest times to meet with Gemini in Gmail - Google Calendar Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/calendar/answer/16865189?hl=en
Product area: calendar
Original file: docs/suggest-times-to-meet-with-gemini-in-gmail-google-calendar-help-9d1dc933.md

# Suggest Times to Meet with Gemini in Gmail — Summary

**Source:** Google Calendar Help  
**URL:** https://support.google.com/calendar/answer/16865189?hl=en

## Key Excerpts

> **Important:** This feature requires an eligible Google Workspace or Google AI plan. [Learn about Gemini features and plans](https://support.google.com/docs/answer/13952129).

> To set up meetings with one or more people in Gmail, use the “Help me schedule” feature when you draft a message or reply. Based on you and your guests’ availability, Gemini in Gmail generates a list of times when you’re all available.

## What the Feature Does

Gemini in Gmail can help schedule meetings directly from an email draft or reply using **Help me schedule**. It checks availability for you and invited guests, then inserts suggested meeting times into the email.

## Requirements

- Requires an eligible **Google Workspace** or **Google AI** plan.
- Available in **Gmail on a computer**.
- Used while composing or replying to an email.

## Insert Suggested Times in a Gmail Message

1. On your computer, go to [Gmail](https://mail.google.com/).
2. Create or reply to an email with one or more recipients.
3. At the bottom of your message, click **Help me schedule**.
   - If you can’t find it, click **More options** → **Help me schedule**.
4. Review the suggested times Gemini generates.
5. Click **Send** at the bottom of the email.

### Tips

- A maximum of **20 guests** can be added to a single schedule.
- If email recipients change after the scheduling card is inserted, the guest list **does not update automatically**.
  - You must **re-insert the schedule**.
- Gemini in Gmail proposes **4 time slots by default**.

## Update the Suggested Times Card

Hover over **Edit in Calendar** and choose one of the available options:

- **Meeting duration:** Change the meeting length.
- **Time range:** Adjust the time range used to find available meeting times.
- **Change to text format:** Send meeting times as plain text instead of a suggestion card.
- **More options:** Insert a booking page from your appointment schedule.

## Update Suggested Times Based on Guest Calendars

You can manually review and refine suggestions based on guest calendars.

### Steps

1. In the suggested times card, click **Edit in Calendar**.
2. In the **“Share your availability”** window, you can:
   - **Check availability:** View availability for any guest calendar you have access to.
   - **Change meeting duration:** Adjust the meeting length.
   - **Add guest time zone:** Include a guest’s time zone.
   - **Add or remove guests:** Modify the guest list on the right side.
     - Suggestions update automatically based on the new guest list.
   - **Review time slots:** Check proposed meeting times when calendars are busy.

### Time Slot Colors

- **Green:** All guests are available.
- **Amber:** One or more guests have a conflict.

If amber conflicts appear, a banner prompts you to manually select time slots. Use the calendar grid to ensure the chosen time works for priority guests.

3. Click **Propose times** at the bottom right.

## Book Time from Someone Else’s Suggested Times

If someone sends you suggested times, you can book a preferred time directly from the email.

Once booked:

- A Google Calendar event is created.
- All guests receive an event invitation.

### Steps

1. In the email, select a suggested time.
2. On the **“Book your preferred time”** page, choose:
   - **Book time for all guests** if there are no conflicts.
   - **View all times on calendar** if there are conflicts or you want another time.
     - Then select a new time slot in the calendar window.

## Gemini Feature Suggestions: Important Disclaimers

- Gemini feature suggestions **don’t represent Google’s views** and should not be attributed to Google.
- Do **not** rely on Gemini features for medical, legal, financial, or other professional advice.
- Gemini may suggest **inaccurate or inappropriate information**.
- Feedback helps improve Gemini’s usefulness and safety.
- Enterprise end users can submit feedback, but are warned not to include personal, sensitive, or confidential information.

## Related Resources

- [Learn about scheduling events in Gmail](https://support.google.com/calendar/answer/16865706)
- [Get started with Google Workspace with Gemini](https://support.google.com/docs/answer/13952129)

## Support and Feedback

The article includes options to:

- Give feedback on the article.
- Report issues such as inaccurate, unclear, missing, irrelevant, or minor-error content.
- Post to the Google Calendar Help Community for additional help.

---

# Document 332: Summarize & analyze responses with Gemini in Google Forms - Google Docs Editors Help

Source URL: https://support.google.com/docs/answer/16231981?hl=en
Product area: forms
Original file: docs/summarize-analyze-responses-with-gemini-in-google-forms-google-docs-editors-help-e594ca19.md

# Summarize & Analyze Responses with Gemini in Google Forms — Summary

**Source:** Google Docs Editors Help  
**URL:** https://support.google.com/docs/answer/16231981?hl=en

---

## Key Excerpts

> **Important:** This feature requires an eligible Google Workspace or Google AI plan.

> You can use Gemini to summarize responses to open text questions in your Google Forms.

> This feature is currently available on desktop and in English.

> You can find generated summaries once there are 3 to 200 responses.

> You can’t generate summaries of quiz responses.

> The generated summaries aren’t saved. You can copy or generate them again.

> Summaries won’t be generated for direct questions, like names and addresses.

> This feature works best with 8–200 responses.

> If your form has more than 200 responses, the theme generation option becomes unavailable. The button appears grayed out and the system displays an error message.

> Because feedback may be human readable, do not submit data that contains personal, confidential, or sensitive information.

> Gemini feature suggestions don’t represent Google’s views, and should not be attributed to Google.

> Don’t rely on Gemini features as medical, legal, financial or other professional advice.

---

## Overview

Google Forms can use **Gemini** to help summarize and analyze open-ended text responses. The feature can:

- Automatically generate summaries for text questions.
- Refresh summaries when new responses arrive.
- Identify and group common themes from responses.
- Export theme analysis into Google Sheets with charts and counts.

This functionality requires an **eligible Google Workspace or Google AI plan** and is currently available only on **desktop** and in **English**.

If the feature is missing and the account belongs to an organization, users should ask their administrator to enable it.

---

## Eligibility & Availability

### Requirements

- Eligible **Google Workspace** or **Google AI** plan.
- Desktop access.
- English language interface/content support.

### Admin Control

If using an organization-managed account and the feature is unavailable, the organization’s admin may need to turn it on.

---

## Use Gemini to Summarize Form Responses

Gemini can summarize responses to **open text questions** in Google Forms.

### Important Limits

- Summaries are available only when there are **3 to 200 responses**.
- Summaries cannot be generated for **quiz responses**.
- Summaries are not generated for direct personal-info style questions, such as:
  - Names
  - Addresses

### Steps to View a Response Summary

1. On a computer, open a form in [Google Forms](https://docs.google.com/forms/).
2. At the top, click **Responses**.
3. In the summary block, summaries for each text question are generated automatically.

### Optional Actions

- Click **Retry** to generate a new response summary.
- Click **Copy** to copy the summary.
- If new responses arrive while the summary is open, click **Refresh with latest responses**.

### Notes

- Generated summaries are **not saved**.
- Users must copy them if they want to retain them.
- They can also regenerate summaries later.

---

## Generate & Refine Themes from Responses

Gemini can identify, group, and analyze common themes in Google Forms text responses using Google Sheets.

### Best-Use Range and Limits

- Works best with **8–200 responses**.
- If a form has **more than 200 responses**, the theme generation option becomes unavailable:
  - The button is grayed out.
  - An error message appears.
- Themes are generated using **AI functions in Google Sheets** and are subject to AI function limits.
- If responses are **fewer than or equal to 8**, or **over 500**, the summary may appear but the theme button is grayed out.

---

## Steps to Generate Themes

1. On a computer, open a form in [Google Forms](https://docs.google.com/forms/).
2. At the top, click **Responses**.
3. Scroll to the text question where you want to generate themes.
4. Below the question’s summary, click **See theme percentages**.
5. To modify generated themes, click **Modify themes**.

### Modify Themes

- To add a theme, click **Add**.
- To delete a theme, click **Delete category** next to the theme name.

### Get Detailed Theme Analysis

Click **See theme percentages** again to open a new Google Sheet.

The generated Google Sheet includes:

- A **pie chart** showing the distribution of themes.
- A **summary response theme count** showing how many responses are associated with each theme.

### Error Handling

If an error occurs in Google Sheets:

1. Open the Sheets side panel.
2. Click **Refresh & Generate**.

---

## Feedback on Gemini Features

Google encourages users to provide feedback if Gemini’s suggestions are:

- Inaccurate
- Unsafe
- Unhelpful
- Inappropriate

Feedback helps improve AI-assisted Workspace features and broader Google AI efforts.

### Privacy Warning

Do **not** submit feedback containing:

- Personal information
- Confidential info

[... summary truncated for context management ...]
